title: NATO Summit Opens in Ankara Amid Escalating Ukraine Strikes, Iran's Farewell, and a Pivotal Week for Global Security
subhead: Zelensky heads to Turkey pressing for air defence after Russia's deadliest Kyiv attack this year, Iran stages a massive funeral for Khamenei signalling resolve for revenge, NATO allies face Trump's spending demands, and OPEC+ cautiously unwinds cuts as Hormuz traffic slowly recovers.

sections.heading: Russia Pounds Kyiv Ahead of NATO Summit as Zelensky Presses for Air Defence
sections.body:
Russia launched 68 missiles and 351 drones at Ukraine overnight, killing at least 20 people and wounding dozens more, according to Ukrainian authorities. The assault on Kyiv — the second large-scale attack on the capital in less than a week — killed at least 14 in the city alone and damaged over a dozen residential buildings, per local officials. The earlier strike late last week killed 31 people, making it the deadliest attack on Kyiv this year.

President Volodymyr Zelensky will use the NATO summit opening Tuesday in Ankara to press allies for Patriot interceptor missiles, arguing that Ukraine failed to intercept a single ballistic missile in Monday's barrage. 'It is simply absurd that, in today's world, production has still not been scaled up to the level actually required to protect people from ballistic terror,' Zelensky said in a video address. He is scheduled to meet US President Donald Trump on the summit sidelines.

Ukraine's deep-strike campaign continues to pressure Russia: Ukrainian drones struck the Omsk refinery in Siberia — roughly 2,700 km from Ukrainian-held territory — one of the longest-ranged strikes of the war, according to Ukraine's General Staff. A Ukrainian strike also knocked out electricity to Russian-controlled Sevastopol in Crimea. Russia's social media is filled with footage of citizens queuing for hours for petrol, a sign the campaign is biting.
sections.whyItMatters: The timing of Russia's assault — just before the NATO summit and after Trump spoke with Putin for 90 minutes — is designed to signal that Moscow will not moderate its campaign. Zelensky's challenge is to convince Trump that Ukraine's deep-strike capability gives it leverage, and that Patriot missiles are the difference between civilians surviving or not.

sections.heading: Iran Buries Khamenei With a Message of Defiance — and a Complicated Succession
sections.body:
Millions lined the streets of Tehran on Monday for the funeral procession of Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, killed in US-Israeli air strikes on 28 February along with four family members including his 14-month-old granddaughter. The ceremony — three days of public mourning culminating in a 10km cortège through the capital — was choreographed to project both grief and resolve. Posters in English singled out Trump as a target; an Iranian messaging app advised supporters to use slogans such as 'Our revenge is inevitable' and 'They will pay. Hard.'

But the funeral also revealed the regime's contradictions. Many Iranians stayed away, hurt by two wars in under a year, inflation around 80%, and January's anti-government protests. 'Many people don't have work and are so unhappy,' one man told the BBC outside a rest station. Iran's new supreme leader, 56-year-old Mojtaba Khamenei, has not appeared in public since the strikes that killed his father and severely injured him — his three brothers stood in at the funeral, his absence conspicuous.

The regime faces a stark trade-off: hardliners oppose the US-Iran deal that cost Khamenei his life, but the government needs sanctions relief to address an economy in freefall. As the BBC's Lyse Doucet reported from Tehran, 'the revolution he preserved was for a world which no longer exists,' in the assessment of Karim Sadjadpour, author of a study on Khamenei's worldview. The burial concludes Thursday at Imam Reza Shrine in Mashhad.
sections.whyItMatters: The funeral was both a genuine outpouring of grief and a carefully staged signal to Washington and Tel Aviv. Mojtaba Khamenei's public absence raises real questions about who is exercising authority in Tehran, and whether the hardline faction or the pragmatists will dominate the next phase.

sections.heading: NATO Summit in Ankara: Spending, Loyalty, and Trump's Shadow
sections.body:
NATO's annual summit opens Tuesday in Ankara with Secretary-General Mark Rutte demanding that all 32 members present 'clear, concrete and credible plans' to reach the 5% GDP defence spending target agreed last year — 3.5% on defence budgets and 1.5% on infrastructure to enable military mobility. 'If one or two of them still have to be convinced, we have ways to do that,' Rutte warned, without elaborating.

US Ambassador to NATO Matthew Whitaker said Trump 'fully expects that all allies will step up immediately and get on the path to five percent.' Trump has also called for 'loyalty' from NATO members, after some declined to allow the use of their bases during the US-Israeli war on Iran. Canada, which recently hit NATO's 2% spending target, announced on Monday it had selected Germany's TKMS to build up to 12 new submarines — the largest defence procurement in Canadian history, estimated at C$20–30 billion for the vessels alone. The Netherlands separately announced defence deals worth over €3 billion, including partnerships with Belgium on air defence and with Britain on naval ships.

Spain has endorsed the 5% target but argues it can meet NATO security requirements without spending that much. Several countries are still struggling to hit the old 2% benchmark. NATO estimates European allies and Canada will invest a combined $258 billion more in 2025 and 2026 than in previous years — but whether that satisfies Washington remains an open question.
sections.whyItMatters: The summit is a test of whether NATO can translate its spending pledges into deliverable plans under intense US pressure. Trump's 'loyalty' framing — linking alliance commitments to whether members supported the Iran campaign — signals that Washington views defence spending through a transactional lens.

sections.heading: Macron Visits Syria, a First for Western Europe Since Assad's Fall
sections.body:
French President Emmanuel Macron arrived in Damascus on Monday evening — the first Western European leader to visit Syria since Bashar al-Assad was ousted in 2024. Macron was greeted at Damascus International Airport by Syrian Foreign Minister Asaad Hassan al-Shaibani. In a post on X, Macron said he had come 'to express France's commitment to the Syrian people. For a sovereign Syria, united in its diversity and at peace with its neighbours.'

Both Macron and Syrian President Ahmed al-Sharaa will travel to Ankara for the NATO summit, where al-Sharaa is expected to have a high-profile meeting with Trump. Macron hosted al-Sharaa in Paris in May 2025 and has pushed European leaders and the US to lift longstanding sanctions on Damascus — most of which have since been removed. The visit comes with Syria still devastated by 13 years of war, requiring hundreds of billions in reconstruction aid.
sections.whyItMatters: Macron's Damascus visit is a symbolic break with Europe's post-2011 posture on Syria. The fact that al-Sharaa — a former HTS leader with past al-Qaeda links — will meet Trump at NATO signals how far Western governments are willing to go to re-engage Syria for regional stability.

sections.heading: OPEC+ Unwinds Cuts for Fifth Straight Month as Hormuz Traffic Slowly Recovers
sections.body:
Seven OPEC+ members — Saudi Arabia, Russia, Iraq, Kuwait, Kazakhstan, Algeria, and Oman — will increase output by 188,000 barrels per day from August, the group announced after a virtual meeting to review market conditions. It is the fifth consecutive monthly increase, continuing the gradual unwinding of production cuts first imposed in 2023.

Brent crude stood at $72 a barrel on Monday, below its pre-war settlement of $72.48 on February 27 — the day before the US and Israel launched strikes on Iran. The drop reflects growing confidence in a sustained end to the Iran conflict and a gradual return to normal shipping in the Strait of Hormuz. Traffic through the strait reached 38 confirmed transits on July 2, down from roughly 130 daily before the war but up sharply from the near-zero levels during the peak of the blockade. Saudi Arabia has more than doubled shipping volume since the June 17 US-Iran memorandum, and Iran has pushed close to 50 million barrels of crude to market since the naval blockade lifted.

Analysts caution the OPEC+ quotas are still largely theoretical while Hormuz throughput remains constrained. 'Actual barrels have been constrained for months by the Strait of Hormuz blockade, falling well short of the quota,' IG analyst Fabien Yip told Al Jazeera. Sparta Commodities' Neil Crosby called the quotas 'essentially meaningless' in the short term.
sections.whyItMatters: Oil prices are back to pre-war levels, but the recovery is fragile. OPEC+ is signalling confidence that Hormuz disruptions are easing, but the gap between quota and actual throughput remains significant. Any reversal in the diplomatic process between the US and Iran could tighten supply again very quickly.

sections.heading: Sudan's el-Obeid Under RSF Drone Siege, Echoes of el-Fasher Disaster
sections.body:
The Rapid Support Forces have escalated drone attacks on the city of el-Obeid, home to over half a million people and a key gateway between the RSF-controlled Darfur region and army-held eastern Sudan. At least 50 civilians have been killed in 10 consecutive days of strikes, according to an international coalition of 28 countries that warned of impending atrocities. Save the Children reports that more than 5,500 children have been newly displaced and a cholera outbreak has produced 300 confirmed cases.

UN Human Rights Chief Volker Turk warned of a humanitarian 'catastrophe,' describing civilians subjected to 'siege-like conditions for 18 months, battered by relentless drone attacks.' Food prices have surged 300%, water prices have doubled, and aid access has shrunk dramatically. The UN Human Rights Council passed a motion on Monday condemning the RSF's escalating attacks.

Analysts see el-Obeid as a repeat of the el-Fasher scenario, where an 18-month RSF siege ended in massacre and confirmed famine last October. 'El-Obeid is really an example of the pervasive use of drones, and how the use of drones then essentially creates the conditions of a siege without having to do an actual full encirclement of the city,' said Leena Badri of the Tahrir Institute for Middle East Policy.
sections.whyItMatters: The pattern is clear from el-Fasher: international warnings, then inaction, then catastrophe. El-Obeid's fall would give the RSF a strategic corridor linking Darfur to central Sudan and reshape the civil war — but the immediate toll is on the half-million civilians trapped inside.

bottomLine: A heavy news day driven by the convergence of multiple security crises on the NATO summit in Ankara. Russia timed its deadliest Kyiv attack of the year to precede the gathering, strengthening Zelensky's case for Patriot missiles but also reminding allies of the war's escalating cost. Iran's regime used Khamenei's funeral to broadcast defiance at the US and Israel, even as its economy and succession remain unresolved. NATO members face Trump's demand for both spending and loyalty. Macron's Damascus visit and al-Sharaa's upcoming Trump meeting mark a rapid Western rehabilitation of Syria's new leadership. OPEC+ is gradually restoring oil output as Hormuz traffic inches upward, but prices and supply remain hostage to diplomatic fragility. In Sudan, el-Obeid faces the same siege-to-massacre trajectory that destroyed el-Fasher — this time under drone bombardment rather than encirclement.
